pageDescription: The insider scoop on some of my projects.
My PHP engine I'm building to run on the .NET CLR
keywords: PHPIL, PHP in C#, PHP on CLR, John Hudson

pageDescription: My favourite project so far. An open source CMS designed for static github pages websites.
A much faster implementation of PHPs Reflection library.
Lightweight PHP Canvas system for SSR and SPA pages with CSP-compliant page state and flexible rendering.
A custom language extension for C#, which allows JSX like syntax to be used in C#, allowing writing HTML in the C#.
My contributions to socialstack core along my employment at 4-roads.
A huge turning point in socialstack's frontend development.
A huge turning point in my career.
Find out the reason why I ended up building this.
content: [{"id":"e7f1561b-f9ad-4dc0-a90a-118f77a6a75e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Before generating TypeScript entities and APIs, JavaScript IntelliSense was almost useless for our frontend developers. Our codebase used a custom Node resolver because pages were rendered as React canvases with a JSON tree describing components. While this setup was necessary for rendering, it caused the language server to run into repeated misdirection, making autocomplete unreliable or nonexistent. Developers could only get hints in limited cases, such as when JSDoc comments were applied to local variables. This workaround was brittle and not scalable for a project of our size.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"9f18f065-649b-4f1f-a5db-6931e1a918b2","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The lack of reliable type information created real friction. Developers spent significant time guessing property names, looking up API endpoints, and verifying payload structures manually. Refactoring was error-prone because there were no static checks for changes between backend data structures and frontend usage. Convincing the team to adopt TypeScript initially met resistance, as it seemed like extra overhead for existing JavaScript workflows.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"622e8a6a-7783-4cc9-8dc0-a5e09b4a380e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Once the team saw how generating typed entities and APIs restored full code completion, static type checking, and autocomplete across the project, the shift became a major productivity win. Tasks that previously required tedious Ctrl+F lookups and trial-and-error coding could now be accomplished confidently with instant IDE hints. What was once a hesitant team quickly embraced the workflow, and the TypeScript-generated API approach became a favorite across the team.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]

pageDescription: Find out the reason why I ended up building this.
content: [{"id":"d04f6c88-8a24-4c5e-8944-8988487722c7","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The goal for the entities was to mirror the same type structure on the frontend as the backend, this would mean code completion becomes a thing in the IDE again, which was a huge productivity bonus, it went from Ctrl+F and symbol lookups while combing through files, to typescript dropping hints about objects. The development team immediately noticed a huge improvement to the developer experience, what was once a hesitant team, decided it was time to make the jump. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"d6bc815c-bf91-48f8-9ac5-6aa4f39858d2","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The next thing to do was collect all fields and properties, in order to follow convention with the types, I had to filter them by modifier flags, BindingFlags.Public | BindingFlags.Instance | BindingFlags.DeclaredOnly, this was to make sure that only fields declared by the entity class, were public, and weren't static. The reason we wanted public only, private fields won't be serialized by JSON serializers, so I skipped anything that wasn't public,I wasn't interested in static members, they're not going to be serialized either, and I used DeclaredOnly to make sure no fields from Content<ID> spilled into the descendant definitions, they should inherit them fields from the generated type for it's parent class. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"3670fe80-6a8e-4456-b263-4cc66a90613d","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Then I wrote the loop iterating the fields and the properties,  they're 2 seperate loops due to GetFields() returning FieldInfo[] whereas GetProperties() returns PropertyInfo[]. Inside that loop, I checked any cases where we could skip a field being generated, this I checked for attributes such as JsonIgnore. After all the checks occured, I had a list of usable fields/ properties I could write to the entity, the next step I had, was converting C# names to TypeScript. All the common number types were cast to number, [typeof(String)] = \"string\", [typeof(Boolean)] = \"boolean\", etc...  From there it was just a case of matching a type to either a preset type, or setting it to a generated type, and importing the reference. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"07ca85ec-bb4a-4ca5-b2f6-57432414e69f","component":"Text","data":{"content":"There was 1 TypeScript file generated per entity (not per type, per entity), I had also created a check, if the type was another content type, it imported that entity, otherwise it generated its own copy of the structure in the same file, every type was resolved fully. The field names where then camelcased, as the JSON response entity was camelcased, with matches JavaScript/TypeScript convention.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"83a55bce-9bc4-4d12-bc33-abfc4e24751e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The last remaining tasks were to get all the virtual fields to generate too, I made these generate as potentially undefined, as in most cases they were, this was useful when typescript then threw up errors if something wasn't undefined/null checked, which meant so many less bugs caused by null objects etc... Virtual fields are linked entities, these are marked at entity level using an attribute called HasVirtualField(\"User\", typeof(User), \"UserId\"), this allowed includes to be a thing, and you can pull in related entities, just by adding the include on the endpoint, I'll talk more about that on the Controller page. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"b03a9e55-9983-4be6-8925-845f0215bb46","component":"Text","data":{"content":"In order to keep memory usage low, and keep generation fast, I used a StringBuilder, the reason I used a StringBuilder was due to string appends create intermediate strings, I also avoided LINQ, LINQ is nice syntax to work with, but can introduce iterator allocations, delegate calls, hidden enumerator structs and even creates additional allocations during some operations, the loops were kept simple, effective and highest performance bounty first, Keeping branching simple allows CPU branch prediction to be more effective, reducing unnecessary jumps and wasted cycles. It also keeps the code easier to reason about, reducing cognitive load for developers maintaining the generator.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]

pageDescription: Find out the reason why I ended up building this.
content: [{"id":"49ef48fb-323e-4d5b-9886-f728a55f4b5b","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Once entity generation was complete, the next step was generating the TypeScript APIs for the controllers. Each controller produced a corresponding TypeScript API class, typically placed alongside the generated entity types for that module. Some controllers did not have associated entities; in those cases an API class was still generated, but without the standard entity operations. For entity-backed controllers, a base AutoController API class was generated first, and entity-specific APIs inherited from it,  a base AutoController API was generated first, and the entity APIs inherited from it. Since TypeScript supports generics in a similar way to C#, it was straightforward to mirror the backend type signatures. This allowed common operations such as list, load, create, update, delete, and listAll to be implemented once in the base API and reused across all generated entity APIs.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"90b32ba8-06a6-4798-8c7b-d5842338d85a","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The generated APIs quickly became one of the most impactful improvements to the codebase. Previously, endpoints were called through a generic webRequest helper that returned loosely typed results. With the generated APIs, calling an endpoint returned a strongly typed Promise matching the backend return type. Each entity module exported a default instantiated API instance. Because ES modules are evaluated once and cached by the module loader, the API instance is created a single time and shared across all imports. This meant the API surface behaved effectively as a singleton while remaining easy to import and use throughout the application.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"98304453-f2f8-4473-a3fa-45161dc31787","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Controller methods were generated by inspecting the backend controllers via reflection. Each method was examined to determine its HTTP verb by looking for ASP.NET attributes such as HttpGetAttribute, HttpPostAttribute, HttpPutAttribute, HttpPatchAttribute, and HttpDeleteAttribute. Route templates defined within these attributes were parsed to detect URL parameters. When parameters were discovered in the route template (for example {id}), they were automatically added to the generated TypeScript method signature. Method parameters were also inspected to determine whether they represented route parameters, query parameters, or request payloads. Primitive values typically mapped to route or query parameters, while complex types were treated as request bodies. Return types were mapped using the same type conversion logic used for entities, translating Task<T> or ValueTask<T> into Promise<T> on the TypeScript side.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"d7e98cea-746a-4503-b9b3-3d13707d4530","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Another important piece was generating support for includes. On the backend, related entities could be requested by passing a comma-delimited list of virtual field names to the endpoint. While functional, this approach relied on manually constructing string values, which made it prone to typos and difficult to discover. To improve this, the generator produced a dedicated includes helper object for each API. Instead of writing arbitrary strings, developers could reference includes through a strongly typed structure exposed via getters. This allowed include chains to be built with full code completion support. For example, rather than writing a string like \"creatorUser,business.parentBusiness\", developers could instead reference userApi.includes.creatorUser or build deeper chains such as userApi.includes.business.parentBusiness.userId. This removed guesswork entirely and made the relationship graph between entities much easier to navigate from the client side.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"69dc64a4-2253-4fe9-a0cc-41ea3f1ba0f3","component":"Text","data":{"content":"To further simplify API consumption on the frontend, we also introduced a small useApi React hook. Prior to this, calling APIs typically required a combination of useState and useEffect, which created a lot of repetitive boilerplate and made simple data loading patterns more verbose than necessary. The useApi hook abstracted this pattern into a single reusable primitive. Instead of manually managing loading state, effect triggers, and cleanup logic, the hook accepts a closure returning a Promise, along with a dependency array that determines when the request should be re-executed.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"51d6ecc2-532b-4b0d-a7ef-a73a584c0e9d","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Internally, the hook behaves similarly to a useEffect that executes an asynchronous function whenever its dependencies change, but it also manages the associated state lifecycle automatically. The promise returned by the closure is resolved and its value is stored internally using useState, with the hook returning the resolved value to the caller. Because the request logic is provided as a closure, it allows the hook to defer execution until the effect runs, while still capturing any variables referenced in the dependency array.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"83d7ca53-3c7e-4eba-afde-3a244e3d1062","component":"Text","data":{"content":"This allowed API calls to be expressed in a very concise way. For example:","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"df5959dd-bd45-482a-88df-de8802f5b395","component":"CodeBlock","data":{"code":"const [user] = useApi(() => userApi.load(userId), [userId]);","language":"tsx","filename":"example side effect:","showCopy":"true","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"51651f37-5d8e-4303-b30a-36e575162041","component":"Divider","data":{"variant":"spacer","size":"md","label":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"2256b273-6a52-45d0-9dbf-15d4254d06bb","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Instead of manually writing several lines of state management and effect logic, a single line expresses both the dependency and the request. The hook automatically re-executes the request when the observed dependencies change, keeping the data in sync with the component state. Combined with the generated typed API clients, this made consuming backend endpoints extremely straightforward while still preserving full type safety.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"3c165c19-00dc-49cf-ac5a-3dad6556507e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"In practice, this significantly reduced boilerplate across the frontend codebase. Most components that needed to fetch data could express their requirements declaratively with a single hook call, while the underlying complexity of state management, effect execution, and promise resolution remained encapsulated inside the hook.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"7f26877c-ca26-4675-bfd4-790bb7381a1e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Together, the generated controllers and entities created a strongly typed contract between the backend and frontend. Endpoint calls were no longer loosely typed requests but well-defined API methods that mirrored the server implementation. This significantly improved discoverability, reduced runtime errors caused by incorrect payloads or response assumptions, and made refactoring much safer as both sides of the contract evolved together.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]

[{"id":"3dc7e155-a455-4574-a2c9-bf1a0bf8c082","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Serialization is a common operation when working with APIs, in most cases data is serialised into the JSON format, in C#, many libraries can perform serialisation, including C#'s System.Text.Json library. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"8bbd1128-8873-43f9-9eda-4e3c1ec97c80","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Objects are serialized by using reflection to iterate fields and properties, appending \"{CamelCase(fieldName)}\" which would convert a field like FirstName => firstName which matches JavaScript conventions on variables and property names. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"2f4f836f-ed46-4bfc-b1e7-a932b6979ed0","component":"Text","data":{"content":"It then recursively serializes the value, if the value is a string, it appends the string, same for number, boolean, null, array or object. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"5cf5f53d-a85e-4001-9cc8-e90513356b99","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Now the problem here is that with whatever library you choose to use, you're making tradeoffs in all of them: Newtonsoft.Json gives you rich features and flexibility but at the cost of higher allocations and slower performance; System.Text.Json is faster and more memory-efficient with lower allocations, but it lacks some of the advanced features and flexibility of Newtonsoft; other high-performance serializers may minimize allocations even further but often require more boilerplate, custom converters, or give up compatibility and ease of use.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"899ce111-a3d7-46f0-a4de-3de5cda78d0e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"On top of this, reflection is inherently slow and expensive when called repeatedly. Using it would also add extra code to maintain and would be limited to types that implement a specific interface, since it requires a shared structure to work. So how can we solve this efficiently? And even if implemented using a StringBuilder or similar buffering, it would still generate a significant number of allocations in high-throughput scenarios.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"effa0f60-3c10-4fad-8dec-0fa55b5e08a0","component":"Text","data":{"content":"We run the app with JIT, since AOT would restrict us to using only System.Text.Json because reflection metadata is not preserved in AOT builds. To serialize, we must use reflection to describe the object shapes. There is no way around this, but we can still apply optimizations to reduce its overhead.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]

pageDescription: Discover why I decided to torture myself building a PHP engine in C#.
content: [{"id":"c81e6992-ef93-4aed-9fab-0ce97ab59172","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Over the last few months, I’ve been obsessed with the .NET CLR. I already understood the basics of IL, but I was itching for a reason to dig deeper. That reason came in the form of a personal challenge: I decided I was going to build a Language Runtime. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"691b548d-3f42-45e7-84ea-3c53244fa03f","component":"Text","data":{"content":"I had previously teamed up with a good friend and ex-colleague on a project called TS.Net, designed to work alongside SocialStack to enable lightning-fast server-side rendering. As we moved to TypeScript and adopted stricter typing, we realized we might be able to drop the V8 engine because, when it comes to memory, V8 is a bit of a hungry beast.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"6fefe76c-e189-4044-a655-ba01cbf89fe2","component":"Text","data":{"content":"We set out on a mission to write a static TypeScript engine, a side project that aimed to run TypeScript directly on the CLR. My colleague led the charge and I played a supporting role, but I wanted to build something like this myself too. The experience and lessons that came with it were exactly what I was after.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"f996eb1e-e9e9-4a85-b750-47d5ef126885","component":"Text","data":{"content":"I knew how complex TypeScript syntax could be, and thought to try a simpler syntax set, and decided since I've already worked with PHP, could I write my own engine, and benchmark it against PHP, see which runs faster. This is how the PHP runtime project came to exist.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]

pageDescription: Discover what steps I took to begin writing my PHP engine.
content: [{"id":"30f4e035-a64f-42b9-94cf-c94772249883","component":"Text","data":{"content":"My first question was simple: which PHP features do I actually want to support? String interpolation? Variable variables? Dynamic class instantiation? The kind of stuff that makes you either a genius or completely lose your mind.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"12637d74-57ff-48bb-b911-d76f3f4ac492","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The work ahead quickly became clear. I needed a lexer to turn source code into a token stream, a parser to turn that into an abstract syntax tree, and finally a way to actually execute it. On top of that, I had to handle syntax errors without making the whole thing explode.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"e8bc388c-b17a-46d9-8430-36799dc47d42","component":"Text","data":{"content":"At this point, I had seen the visitor pattern before but had never really understood its purpose or why it was used. Later in the project I came to realise: I've been a absolute sausage ignoring it.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"767b7df1-08d0-4438-9154-4249f106a96a","component":"Divider","data":{"variant":"line","size":"md","label":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"726d6860-29c7-4a6b-8d71-862a28aa3177","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Code Lexer","tag":"h2","align":"left","weight":"bold","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"20px","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"c29d4fce-bbc0-4ebc-ab6b-8fedc92e8ed0","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Beginning with the first challenge: code lexing and tokenizing. The file would be loaded as a string, and I had been planning how to push myself to build a tokenizer that used as little memory as possible. Using Span<char> allowed me to avoid allocations almost everywhere. Since a Span cannot be captured off the stack, I could take advantage of the stack’s fast 1MB memory block. By loading the source into a string, calling .AsSpan(), and iterating over it one character at a time, I could build the token stream efficiently and with minimal overhead.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"19a0c1d1-6f46-4020-948d-cb49dd88fd70","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Next, I moved on to designing the token data structure. Should I use a record, a class, or a struct? Classes always live on the heap, structs usually live on the stack, and while there are some edge cases where structs end up on the heap, I was careful to avoid those. Records can add extra overhead in certain scenarios, especially with copying and equality checks. In the end, I decided to go with a contiguous struct. It is fast, predictable, and memory-friendly, just the way I like it.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"4df35fd8-84d7-4269-8fcb-c86b64f1d4ff","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The first fields I decided to capture were the range start and range end. Instead of doing string comparisons and creating unnecessary string allocations, I already had access to the source via a Span<char>. I could grab the sequence only when I actually needed it. For most tokens, apart from literals, identifiers, and variables, I didn’t even need the token value. This also helped avoid unnecessary GC pressure from reference types lingering around like unwanted party guests.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"4a3978eb-9b8a-4cce-a126-9733082b333a","component":"Text","data":{"content":"RangeStart and RangeEnd are signed Int32s, which means four bytes each. That puts the struct at eight bytes so far. I still needed a way to mark the token type for the AST parser. I went with a byte-backed enum since there would never be more than 255 token kinds. That brings the struct’s total size to nine bytes, though CPUs love alignment, so it would likely be padded to 12 bytes. Efficient, predictable, and ready to churn through source code without complaint. The enum route also allows for nice pattern matching which JIT can inline incredibly well, when the JIT starts moving the tokeniser through JIT tiers, parsing happens in sub ms time. #BigUpRyuJIT","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"a6ba6496-7028-4053-8edc-61a9cc98e40e","component":"Divider","data":{"variant":"line","size":"md","label":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"10b7b547-a745-41f6-afcd-46bb76fc60d4","component":"Text","data":{"content":"AST Parser/ Syntax Tree","tag":"h2","align":"left","weight":"bold","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"20px","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"47583a1b-a094-4d17-9e5a-c808e4f58cfc","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The next thing I had to plan, once the tokenizer was complete, was parsing the AST. My friend and ex-colleague recommended using productions, which let you define grammar rules and then combine them to match more complex sequences. I wanted to challenge myself here as well. My plan was to use Span<Token> with in, just like I did with the source span. Using in signals that the span itself won’t be reassigned in the method, which avoids unnecessary copies and gives a small performance boost while keeping the parser efficient.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"f768c461-5008-4178-a43f-1b371b0dae0c","component":"Text","data":{"content":"To use combinators effectively, I needed to track the current pointer in the token array. Every grammar rule had to generate a valid AST node and advance the pointer. It also needed to check for the longest match first, handle operator precedence according to PHP’s guide, and be able to roll back when necessary.  To manage all of this efficiently, I decided to create a ref struct. Using a ref struct has a few big advantages. First, it cannot escape to the heap, so all of its state stays on the stack, which avoids unnecessary allocations and reduces GC pressure. Second, it allows me to pass both the source span and the token span by reference without copying the spans themselves. Every time a token matched or the parser rewound, the struct could be updated in place, keeping parsing lightning fast and memory efficient.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"21f4d7d6-21b8-48d7-a7d1-f87abd6c3bd1","component":"Text","data":{"content":"To keep the combinators as modular as possible, I used a static Parser class. Inside it, a switch on the current token kind allowed me to skip iterating through every grammar rule, creating a hot path that the JIT could aggressively optimize. This made recursive descent parsing straightforward and naturally led to a Pratt parser for handling operator precedence efficiently. Once the JIT warmed up and started moving code through its tiers, parsing happened in sub-millisecond time, even for large token streams. Again, #BigUpRyuJIT.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"320fcfad-cc46-4113-8698-ae5a948f51a9","component":"Divider","data":{"variant":"line","size":"md","label":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"6337bc1e-d9a5-48a1-80c2-280744c685b7","component":"Text","data":{"content":"IL Production/ Node Visitors.","tag":"h2","align":"left","weight":"bold","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"20px","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"5100f040-dcc8-457c-beca-21dd71cadefd","component":"Text","data":{"content":"This is where it got really exciting. At this point, I wasn’t sure how I was going to take the AST and actually execute it. I knew I needed to generate IL, but the path forward was unclear. My first thought was to recursively iterate over the AST’s children and handle execution that way. I tried it, but no matter how I structured it, I didn’t like how it ran or how it “felt” in code.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"53a852fe-3bd1-4fa7-b085-ae6aae6f0c01","component":"Text","data":{"content":"I spoke with my friend and ex-colleague, and he told me about the visitor pattern and how it could simplify execution. I added it a little reservedly at first, but as I dug deeper, I realized how much visitors could do: type detection, dead code removal, and more. That’s when I got hooked and knew this was the right approach.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"5f94bc18-3b34-41b1-815a-6959430ef561","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The final step was adding the ILGeneration visitor. I knew IL could be brutal, and InvalidProgramException(\"The CLR detected an Invalid Program\") had haunted me more times than I could count. To make the process manageable, I created an ILGenerator wrapper. At first, I considered extending ILGenerator, but it is sealed, so that was not possible. Instead, I took a facade approach: I built a class with the same call signatures as the original ILGenerator that also logged every instruction as it was emitted.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"d47a2c33-05b9-4573-9c37-8999b9948125","component":"Text","data":{"content":"This wrapper allowed me to track the evaluation stack at every step, see exactly which opcodes were being pushed, popped, or boxed, and understand how branches, locals, and method calls were interacting. By capturing the sequence of emitted instructions, I could detect mismatched stack states, incorrect opcode order, and other subtle IL errors that normally only manifest as runtime exceptions. This approach transformed IL generation from an opaque, error-prone process into a precise, debuggable workflow, making it far easier to reason about the generated code and fix mistakes efficiently. It was here that visitors finally clicked, it was like this black box just exploded and revealed its contents, I could visualise the entire process from parsing to execution.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]

pageDescription: After being made redundant, I realised I have repositories, but not an actual portfolio, then...
content: [{"id":"4e785022-2826-4489-bcaa-198a7bb38e2c","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Just over a week before writing this, I was made redundant. I realised that getting back into the job market meant I needed something to give me credibility. I had been scrolling through LinkedIn and noticed a lot of developers were being let go in large numbers. I thought about how I could stand out and maybe even help others in the same position. That is how CodeFolio came to be.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"0cb260e3-0fa7-4517-b18b-34bcc2f647c6","component":"Text","data":{"content":"I wanted a portfolio hosted on my GitHub account, free via GitHub Pages, where I could link to my repositories, explain my thought process, showcase my work, and maintain full control over both content and design. It was about owning my own data, presenting my work my way, and creating a resource that could genuinely set me apart.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"531f2d96-b1fc-44f3-971b-c191f35cabcd","component":"Text","data":{"content":"I also realised that if I built this portfolio builder as a standalone project and used it for my own portfolio, I could validate its use case while giving others a simple, ready made way to showcase their work. Another idea struck me as I thought about the future. Large language models are increasingly capable of understanding structured data like JSON. By designing CodeFolio to store content in a structured JSON format, it becomes not only easy to render and display but also easy for AI tools to parse.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"b6dd1340-15bd-44fc-93a7-43df3b38e5e8","component":"Text","data":{"content":"This allows other developers or automated tools to quickly analyse a portfolio, extract key projects, and generate summaries or insights. It opens the door to smarter recommendations, automated highlights, and better discoverability. By making the data machine readable, portfolios are futureproofed and ready to integrate with the next generation of AI driven tools.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]

pageDescription: Using express to setup a http server that acted...
content: [{"id":"46593761-751e-43bf-b31f-2a2e09254b19","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The first thing I knew I needed to do was set up a server. I didn’t really want to write my own router. It is an easy enough task, but very boring. Instead, I chose to use Express, which handles dynamic routes and serves static directories, giving me everything I needed.  I wanted this application to be service-first, so I decided to emit decorator metadata and use reflect-metadata. This allowed me to use decorators to mark classes with @Service, @Controller, and @Entity, each serving a distinct purpose. This approach made it easy to start building the admin functionality.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"b8545844-92ef-4308-8cd9-9ad94f3021df","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Next, I created a method to collect all the services and start them. I knew that some services would need to perform asynchronous tasks, so we standardized the onInit method to use async/await. Making the service initialization itself asynchronous allowed me to await each service as it was set up, ensuring all services were properly initialized before moving on.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"415cf440-1ea0-4453-a2a7-4a22a199c84f","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The first service I implemented was the HTTP service. Its role was to collect all controllers and identify methods decorated with @Get, @Post, @Put, @Patch, or @Delete. Each decorator accepted a single argument, the endpoint path, which was combined with the controller's base route to determine the full route. These routes were then registered with Express. All endpoints were implemented as asynchronous functions, receiving req, res, and any URL parameters. This design enabled flexible handling of requests and simplified the creation of custom pages and dynamic endpoints.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]

pageDescription: Discover my reasoning when adding React functionality to CodeFolio
content: [{"id":"fc117dac-769e-426e-bc23-c2c1a98c8f1b","component":"Text","data":{"content":"For the UI layer, React was the obvious choice. It has strong adoption among developers, and I was already comfortable working with it. I also planned to implement a canvas-style page builder similar to SocialStack, which makes page scaffolding extremely straightforward. The long term goal was to support a drag-and-drop editor, so choosing a component-based framework aligned well with that direction.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"a7225c65-ef48-4291-9f1a-88ded51aaf5e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Supporting this workflow required a fast and flexible build system, so I chose ESBuild. Performance was a major factor, but just as important was simplifying the developer experience. My goal was to make creating plugins, components, or themes as frictionless as possible, allowing developers to extend the system with minimal setup.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"5bc2e892-0ada-4b97-8fcf-20b6fe79a494","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Once this infrastructure was in place, the next step was implementing the page system. This began with the WebSocket component, which integrates with the development WebSocket server. It listens for reload events and triggers a refresh when they are received. It also monitors the connection state and displays a message if the WebSocket disconnects, providing immediate feedback during development.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"15588915-5993-41c5-a2d4-926930a1a455","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Inside this component sits a configuration provider that exposes a hook called useModuleConfig. This hook allows components to access module configuration at runtime in a consistent way. The configuration is stored as JSON within the build directory and loaded by the application when needed.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"301050e8-aca5-4a1f-85fb-86d3fdf7c92e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Configuration itself is managed through the administrative UI. The admin interface provides controls for modifying module settings, and these changes are persisted to the JSON configuration file that the frontend reads at runtime. By exposing this data through the provider, components can retrieve their configuration through useModuleConfig without needing to know where or how the configuration is stored.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"f303e218-c5c9-40f3-a033-a8eeb3fc31a9","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The final piece was the router component. It reads the current pathname from the browser and resolves it to a JSON page definition stored in the content directory. The router constructs the path dynamically based on the requested URL.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"f37e3864-eeb4-425b-ba93-33719f38085b","component":"Text","data":{"content":"For example, if the current pathname is /page/254, the router attempts to load the corresponding file from /content/page/254.json. If the file exists, the router loads the page definition and renders the associated components. If the JSON file cannot be found or fails to load, the router falls back to rendering a 404 page.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"6de1f6c4-e827-4156-b174-f85a92412a9c","component":"Text","data":{"content":"This approach allows routes to be defined purely through content files, keeping the routing layer simple while enabling pages to be managed through the content system.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]
